content.proposedH1: The Story of Yerevan Sac: An Armenian Family Bringing the Caucasus to Carmichael
content.metaDescription: Meet the family behind Yerevan Sac — Carmichael's only authentic Armenian-Georgian restaurant. Our story, our chefs, and why we serve the food of two countries at one table.

h2Sections.heading: Why 'Yerevan Sac' — and Why Both Armenian and Georgian
contentBullets: The name: Yerevan is the capital of Armenia — the city our family is from. 'Sac' is our local nod to Sacramento.
We serve both Armenian and Georgian cuisine because the Caucasus has always shared a table — the borders between food, language, and family in this region are softer than any map suggests.
Our menu reflects what is actually cooked at home in Yerevan and Tbilisi — not the Mediterranean-Greek hybrid most American restaurants offer.
We are family-owned, family-run, and family-cooked. Recipes come from grandmothers, not consultants.

h2Sections.heading: The Kitchen — Meet the People Behind the Mangal
contentBullets: Our pit master runs the mangal grill — the open-flame Armenian grill that produces every kebab, every lamb chop, every quail on the menu. He has been doing this for decades.
Our khinkali maker pleats every dumpling by hand each morning. Twenty pleats minimum, never less.
Our khachapuri baker shapes each cheese boat by hand. The dough rises overnight. The cheese is the right cheese.
We name our chefs because Google asks who you are — and the people in our kitchen are the answer.

h2Sections.heading: Why Carmichael — and What 'Authentic' Actually Means Here
contentBullets: Sacramento has one of the largest Armenian diaspora communities on the West Coast. Yerevan Sac is where that community comes to eat the food they grew up with.
We didn't 'adapt' the menu for the American palate. We trust that great food translates without softening.

Dishes like Kubdari (Svanetian Georgian meat bread), Chakapuli (Georgian lamb-tarragon stew), Tjvjik (Armenian liver and heart), and Pkhali (Georgian vegetable spreads) are on our menu because they belong on a Caucasian table — not because they test well in focus groups.
If a guest hasn't tried something before, we will tell them what it is, how it is eaten, and why it matters.

h2Sections.heading: The Cross-Cultural Caucasus
contentBullets: The Caucasus has always been a crossroads. You'll see this on our menu — Eastern European Borscht, Soviet-era Napoleon pastry, even a Korean-style beef dish that reflects the Koryo-saram (Soviet Koreans) who settled across the region.
These dishes are not fusion — they are how Caucasian families have actually cooked for generations.
Armenian cuisine leans on grilled meats, fresh herbs, and bright finishes (lemon, pomegranate, sumac).
Georgian cuisine leans on bread, cheese, and walnut. Together, they cover the entire spectrum of what a Caucasian feast looks like.
